primary_diagnosis.name: Acute coeliac/functional gut flare after gluten exposure (dietary indiscretion), surgical abdomen not fully excludable remotely

pathophysiology_summary: A large gluten load in coeliac/gluten-sensitive gut provokes an osmotic and inflammatory response with fermentation, gaseous distension and painful smooth-muscle spasm — producing dramatic bloating and colic that can mimic mechanical small-bowel obstruction. The reassuring discriminators are colicky (not constant) pain, absence of bilious/faeculent vomiting, continued (even if difficult) passage of wind, a soft-sounding abdomen and no localised peritonism. However, complete obstipation with distension, bilious vomiting, constant severe or localised pain, fever, or pain out of proportion would signal obstruction, appendicitis or mesenteric ischaemia — none of which can be excluded by abdominal examination remotely. Safe management therefore couples symptomatic relief with a strict surgical-abdomen safety-net and a low threshold for in-person assessment.

differential.evidence_basis: Colicky bloating after a large pasta meal with a history of episodic symptoms and pending coeliac serology.
Distension with difficulty passing wind requires screening for obstipation and bilious vomiting; cannot examine remotely.
Screen for RLQ migration, fever and anorexia; absent here.
Pain out of proportion with cardiovascular risk would suggest ischaemia; not present in a young patient with proportionate pain.

Colicky (not constant) pain, no bilious vomiting and still passing some wind (DI-001), no fever or RLQ localisation (DI-002), onset after a gluten meal with prior episodes (DI-003) — a functional/coeliac flare.
Still passing wind, no bilious vomiting — complete obstruction not currently supported, but retain a strict safety-net.
No RLQ pain, fever or anorexia.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: antispasmodic and simple measures (heat, gentle mobilisation), explicit obstruction/appendicitis/ischaemia safety-net, and a low threshold for in-person review because the abdomen cannot be palpated remotely. Neither a reflex midnight ED/CT nor unscreened reassurance is acceptable.

red_flags.description: Complete obstipation (no wind/motion) with bilious or faeculent vomiting (bowel obstruction)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Mechanical obstruction can lead to strangulation and perforation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed obstruction progresses to ischaemia, perforation and peritonitis.

red_flags.description: Constant severe or localised (RLQ) pain, fever, peritonism, or pain out of proportion
red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests appendicitis, peritonitis or mesenteric ischaemia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed surgical abdomen leads to perforation/sepsis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Sending every dramatic bloating flare for a midnight ED/CT without screening the discriminating features.
Reassuring a distended, obstipation-adjacent abdomen that cannot be examined remotely, missing early obstruction.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Auscultation of bowel sounds (tinkling vs normal)
Percussion note and any imaging (erect CXR/AXR/CT)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Antispasmodic and simple measures with a strict surgical-abdomen safety-net and low threshold for same-day in-person review; ED now if obstruction/peritonism features present.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Either extreme: an unnecessary midnight CT for a functional flare, or a falsely reassured early obstruction.
